petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented artist, musician, and entertainer (trombone player, arranger, and musical director) who sought classification as an employment-based immigrant of extraordinary ability under INA § 203(b)(1)(A). He participated in various Latin/salsa musical groups including Orquesta Brava, Grupo Sarao, Nueva Energia, and Fruko y sus Tesos, and claimed his biggest accomplishment was as trombone player and arranger on the song 'Eres Tu' which charted on the top 50 salsa charts. The petition was filed on July 27, 2007, and denied by the Director of the Texas Service Center on January 12, 2009.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed eligibility based on his participation as a trombone player at the 2007 Latin Billboard Music Awards. He submitted a copy of his badge from the event along with several photographs with captions claiming that they reflected winners from the event.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i) requires documentation of the alien's 'receipt' of prizes or awards. The petitioner conceded he did not receive any major awards individually. Merely submitting documentation reflecting participation at an awards event without evidence demonstrating receipt of any prizes or awards is insufficient. Furthermore, the AAO noted the plain language requires receipt of more than one award or prize, so even if his participation qualified, he would have established eligibility for only one award.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed membership with The Recording Academy (TRA) and the Columbian Association of Recording Artists and Composers (CARAC). He submitted a letter dated January 12, 2007 from TRA congratulating him on becoming a member, a copy of his TRA membership card, and a second letter from Mr. Crilly stating that voting members must demonstrate 'their creative and/or technical involvement in a minimum number of nationally-released musical recordings.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held the petitioner must show the association requires outstanding achievement as an essential condition for admission. Membership requirements based on employment/activity in a field, minimum education or experience, test scores, GPA, recommendations, or payment of dues do not satisfy this criterion. The overall prestige of the association (e.g., the Grammy Awards) is not determinative. The TRA criteria of 'creative and/or technical involvement in a minimum number of nationally-released musical recordings' is not tantamount to outstanding achievements, and the petitioner failed to establish that membership is judged by recognized national or international experts. The petitioner submitted no documentary evidence establishing his membership with CARAC or its requirements. Additionally, the regulation requires membership in more than one qualifying association.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Six items: (1) An article 'Fruko Puts...Measure of Colombian Salsa,' July 22, 2006, Chicago Sun-Times; (2) uncertified/partial translation of 'Kenny Quintero and His Band Take the Genre Everywhere,' February 3, 2007, unidentified author, El Sentinel; (3) uncertified/partial translation of 'Grupo Sarao Continues with Strength,' unidentified date/author/source; (4) uncertified/partial translation of advertisement 'Claudia Diez & Tono 40,' unidentified date/author/source; (5) uncertified/partial translation of 'The Stars of Calle 8,' www.univision.com; (6) uncertified/partial translation of 'Kenny Quintero in the Miami Carnival,' www.univision.com.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The regulation requires published material to be 'about' the petitioner relating to his work. Articles that mention a group in which the petitioner performs are insufficient. Item 1 never mentions the petitioner. Item 2 mentions him once as one of the arrangers and appears to be about Grupo Sarao and its song 'Eres Tu.' Item 3 is a newspaper advertisement that never mentions the petitioner, and the AAO was not persuaded an advertisement equates to 'published material.' Items 4-6 never mention the petitioner, with items 5 and 6 consisting of only one sentence. Only one of six articles mentions the petitioner's name. The petitioner also failed to include dates/authors and certified full translations for items 2-6. The petitioner failed to demonstrate the material was published in professional or major trade publications or other major media, and international accessibility of www.univision.com alone is not a realistic indicator of 'major media.'

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner relied on his TRA membership card reflecting that he is a 'Voting Member' of The Recording Academy (GRAMMY/Latin GRAMMY Awards).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The petitioner submitted only a membership card reflecting voting member status but failed to establish that he has ever voted for anything, let alone 'as a judge of the work of others.' He had only been a voting member since January 2007, six months prior to filing. Merely submitting documentation reflecting the ability to vote is insufficient without documentation reflecting that he actually judged the work of others. The petitioner failed to establish the duties and responsibilities of a voting member—for example, voting on administrative functions like the date of an awards ceremony would not qualify, whereas voting on musical performers (e.g., best group of the year) would qualify.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a list reflecting his participation as a trombone player, arranger, and/or studio director for ten artists, along with copies of CD covers. He claimed participation on records of bands including Orquesta Brava, Grupo Sarao, Nueva Energia, and Fruko y sus Tesos as arranger, musician, and/or musical director. Full and certified translations of the CD covers were not submitted.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The regulation requires contributions to be both original AND of major significance. While CD covers reflect that the petitioner performed on or arranged the songs, he failed to demonstrate that his performances or participation were of major significance to the field. Merely submitting documentation reflecting participation as a musician or producer on several CDs is insufficient without evidence reflecting that the petitioner's work has influenced or impacted the field of music. The lack of supporting documentary evidence gave the AAO no basis to gauge the significance of his contributions.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted flyers, posters, ads, and newspaper articles announcing some of his concerts as part of different orchestras, including performances with Orchestra Brava, Claudia Diez y Tono 40, and Fruko y sus Tesos. He also included photos of himself during presentations, including participation in the Calle 8 Festival in Miami, FL. Non-certified translations were included.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held this criterion relates to the visual arts. It is inherent to the field of music and entertaining to perform on stage or in public; therefore, not every stage performance is an artistic exhibition or showcase. The regulations establish separate criteria, especially for those whose work is in the performing arts. The petitioner's performances and concerts are more relevant to the 'commercial successes in the performing arts' criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(x).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed eligibility based on his participation with a musical organization called 'Julymar productions,' where he stated he served as Musical Director of the producer and the leading musician. He submitted a supporting letter.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director determined the documentary evidence, a letter submitted by the petitioner, failed to establish that he performed in a leading or critical role. On appeal, the petitioner failed to contest the director's decision or offer additional arguments, so the AAO did not further discuss this criterion on appeal. The AAO also noted the plain language of the regulation requires more than one leading or critical role.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted uncertified translations of royalty payments from several entities, including Codiscos (described by the petitioner as the biggest and most important record company in Colombia), Disco Tienda Latina, and Col Music, for his participation on CDs.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: While the evidence reflects that the petitioner received royalty payments, he failed to submit any documentary evidence comparing his royalty payments to others in the field to establish that he commanded 'significantly high remuneration for services.' Merely submitting documentation reflecting a salary or remuneration without evidence establishing significantly high remuneration compared to others in the field is insufficient.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed the song 'Eres Tu' by Grupo Sarao remained on the top of the US charts from December 2001 to November 2002. He submitted chart statistics: (1) Number 6 - Top 50 Salsa, December 1, 2001; (2) Number 3 - Top 50 Salsa, January 21, 2002; (3) Number 11 - Top 40 Salsa, January 21, 2002; (4) Number 21 - Top 40 Radio and Musica, September 29, 2002; (5) Number 34 - Top 50 Salsa, November 18, 2002. On appeal, he submitted a printout from www.artistdirect.com proving his participation as arranger, musical director, and trombonist.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The regulation requires evidence of commercial successes in the form of 'box office receipts' or 'sales.' The petitioner's submission of top 40 and 50 radio spins/chart positions of a single song does not equate to evidence of box office receipts or sales. There is no evidence showing box office receipts for performances or sales of songs/compact disks. Furthermore, the plain language requires more than one commercial success; even if 'Eres Tu' qualified, he would have only established one.

outcome_reasoning: The appeal was dismissed. The petitioner failed to establish eligibility for any of the ten regulatory criteria, of which at least three are required. In the final merits determination, the AAO found the petitioner's accomplishments fell far short of establishing he is 'one of that small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field of endeavor' with 'sustained national or international acclaim.' A single song reaching number three on the top 50 salsa charts approximately five years before filing was insufficient to establish sustained acclaim. The lack of published material about him, the absence of extensive documentation (including numerous uncertified and partial translations violating 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3)), the failure to demonstrate he had ever actually voted despite TRA voting membership, and the absence of comparative salary evidence all demonstrated he had not achieved the requisite level of extraordinary ability. Comparable evidence under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4) was not available because the petitioner failed to show the regulatory criteria did not readily apply to his occupation as a musician/entertainer.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Spencer Enterprises, Inc. v. United States, 229 F. Supp. 2d 1025 (E.D. Cal. 2001), aff'd, 345 F.3d 683 (9th Cir. 2003)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1972)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The petitioner submitted numerous non-certified English language translations, partial translations, and foreign language documents without any English translations, in violation of 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), which requires a 'full English language translation' certified by a competent translator. Because of this non-compliance, the AAO could not determine whether the evidence supported the petitioner's claims, and the evidence was not accorded any weight in the proceeding. No fraud or misrepresentation findings were made.
